nativeRange.habitat: Shallow rocky reefs at roughly 1–5 m depth, where individuals shelter inside empty barnacle shells, polychaete worm tubes, and abandoned tube-mollusc shells, leaving only the head exposed.

diet.notes: A zooplanktivore in the wild — generally observed darting from its hole to snatch passing zooplankton. In captivity has been reported to readily accept small meaty foods: vitamin-enriched frozen mysis and brine shrimp, finely chopped crustacean flesh, marine pellets, and similar prepared foods. Multiple small meals per day are commonly advised, since this species feeds opportunistically on whatever drifts past its perch.

avoidWith: other small territorial blennies in confined spaces

summary: A tiny Eastern Pacific tube-blenny that lives wedged inside empty barnacle shells and worm tubes, darting out to grab passing zooplankton. Generally considered reef-safe, peaceful, and easy to feed, but is a strong jumper and benefits from a sealed lid.
careNotes:
The Panamic Barnacle Blenny (Acanthemblemaria hancocki, family Chaenopsidae — the pike-, tube- and flagblennies) is a tiny, charismatic tube-dweller from the rocky reefs of the tropical Eastern Pacific, ranging from El Salvador and Costa Rica south to Panama and Ecuador. In the wild it shelters inside empty barnacle shells, polychaete worm tubes, and discarded tube-mollusc shells, typically at 1–5 m depth, peeking out to ambush drifting zooplankton.

In the aquarium it is generally considered an excellent beginner reef fish: small (around 4–5 cm), peaceful toward unrelated tankmates, fully reef-safe with corals and invertebrates, and undemanding in terms of diet once settled. It spends almost all of its time perched in a chosen crevice, racing out to snatch food from the water column and returning to its hole — so a well-established aquascape with plenty of small holes, empty shells, and tight crevices is the most important element of its setup. Providing a few empty barnacle shells or short lengths of rigid tubing is generally appreciated.

Like most blennies and chaenopsids, it has been reported to jump out of open-topped aquariums, so a tightly fitting lid is generally advised. Conspecifics are commonly kept together in larger systems but can squabble over preferred holes in tight quarters; one specimen per small tank, or several in a tank with abundant, dispersed crevices, tends to work best. Feed small, frequent meaty foods — mysis, brine, finely chopped seafood, marine pellets — multiple times per day, since the species' opportunistic ambush strategy means it rarely eats large amounts at a single sitting.

Cross-source notes: taxonomy — LiveAquaria lists this fish under family Blenniidae, which is incorrect; FishBase places it in Chaenopsidae, order Blenniiformes, which is followed here. Adult size — LiveAquaria states ~2 in (~5 cm) and FishBase reports 4.5 cm TL max; written as 4–5 cm. Temperature — LiveAquaria and Quality Marine give 72–78°F (22–26°C), while FishBase's modeled preferred range from wild Eastern Pacific habitat is 26.9–29.1°C; averaged to a 23–27°C aquarium range that respects both the hobby norms and the warm tropical native habitat. Minimum tank — LiveAquaria and Quality Marine both cite 10 US gal (~38 L), with other hobby sources suggesting 20 US gal (~75 L) is more comfortable; per the playbook's larger-tank rule, biased up to 75 L since the species is a perched watcher that benefits from an aquascape with many dispersed crevices rather than a tiny nano cube.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Used for hobby husbandry figures: 10 US gal minimum, 2 in (~5 cm) adult size, 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12, care level Easy, peaceful temperament, carnivore diet on meaty frozen foods, reef-safe. Note: LiveAquaria's family listing of Blenniidae is incorrect; FishBase's Chaenopsidae assignment is used in taxonomy.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-confirmed scientific name Acanthemblemaria hancocki, correct family Chaenopsidae (pike-, tube- and flagblennies), order Blenniiformes, max length 4.5 cm TL, depth 1–5 m, marine reef-associated, native range Costa Rica and Panama (Eastern Central Pacific), habitat in empty worm and tube mollusk tubes on rocky reefs, modelled preferred temperature 26.9–29.1°C, planktivorous feeding, IUCN Least Concern, harmless to humans.

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Used as a third source to triangulate temperature, tank size, and behavior. Confirmed 10 gal minimum, 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, alkalinity 8–12 dKH, ~2 in adult size, reef-safe, easy care, and added the jumping/escape-risk warning: 'provide an aquarium with a tight fitting lid as Blennies may jump'.
